messages.author: swdrumm
messages.text: TL;DR - Use dedicated tx windows based on GPS timing to prevent nodes from transmitting on-top of one another. Proposal: Like APRS, MeshCore transmits with only minimal regard for packet collisions. In contrast, amateur radio HF digital modes like JS8Call use transmit windows based on GPS timing to ensure that stations don't talk over one another - basically, a computerized version of a "talking stick". Adding this functionality to MeshCore would minimize collisions and increase overall network throughput in regions with a large number of nodes. Constraints: Nodes will require either GPS or a real-time clock and the ability to receive time corrections from other nodes. Maybe add another node type (e.g. "MASTER CLOCK") just for timing sync?
messages.content_html: TL;DR - Use dedicated tx windows based on GPS timing to prevent nodes from transmitting on-top of one another. Proposal: Like APRS, MeshCore transmits with only minimal regard for packet collisions. In contrast, amateur radio HF digital modes like JS8Call use transmit windows based on GPS timing to ensure that stations don't talk over one another - basically, a computerized version of a "talking stick". Adding this functionality to MeshCore would minimize collisions and increase overall network throughput in regions with a large number of nodes. Constraints: Nodes will require either GPS or a real-time clock and the ability to receive time corrections from other nodes. Maybe add another node type (e.g. "MASTER CLOCK") just for timing sync?

messages.author: swdrumm
messages.text: Seems like this is a real problem for scaling large networks and it's been solved before (LTE / 5G): "Regarding specific transmission windows and time slicing, modern networks do incorporate time-based resource division, though it's integrated with frequency division for efficiency. In LTE, time is sliced into frames (10 ms), subframes (1 ms, also called Transmission Time Intervals or TTIs), and slots (0.5 ms), with data transmitted in orthogonal frequency-division multiple access (OFDMA) resource blocks that combine time and frequency. This allows precise scheduling where users are assigned specific time-frequency slots to avoid interference. 5G enhances this with flexible numerology, supporting shorter TTIs (down to 0.125 ms mini-slots) for ultra-low latency applications, enabling finer-grained time slicing. Network slicing in 5G can also allocate dedicated time resources per slice, but this is more about virtual partitioning than pure time-division multiple access (TDMA) seen in older 2G networks. Overall, these mechanisms ensure efficient spectrum use without fixed "windows" per user, adapting in real time via base station schedulers." (edited)

This allows precise scheduling where users are assigned specific time-frequency slots to avoid interference. 5G enhances this with flexible numerology, supporting shorter TTIs (down to 0.125 ms mini-slots) for ultra-low latency applications, enabling finer-grained time slicing. Network slicing in 5G can also allocate dedicated time resources per slice, but this is more about virtual partitioning than pure time-division multiple access (TDMA) seen in older 2G networks. Overall, these mechanisms ensure efficient spectrum use without fixed "windows" per user, adapting in real time via base station schedulers." (edited)

embeds_html:
Self-organized time-division multiple access
Self-organized time-division multiple access (STDMA or SOTDMA) is a channel access method designed by Håkan Lans, based on time-division multiplexing. The term "self-organized" describes the manner in which time slots are assigned to users. Time-division multiple access (TDMA) divides a channel into frames, which furthermore are subdivided into...
